did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel deep learning methodology for skin lesion prediction and classification. Specifically, it employs U-Net for segmentation, extracts features using GLCM and RGB histograms, and utilizes a Modified Long Short-Term Memory (MLSTM) model optimized by a hybrid metaheuristic algorithm (SC-STBO) to classify skin lesions into seven categories using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving a novel deep learning methodology (Modified Long Short-Term Memory model optimized by a hybrid SC-STBO algorithm) for skin lesion prediction and classification using the ISIC dataset. The paper details specific methods (U-Net segmentation, GLCM feature extraction), results (accuracy of 0.9931, recall of 0.9825), and comparisons with traditional techniques, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesion Prediction and Classification Using Innovative Modified Long Short‑Term Memory‑Based Hybrid Optimization Algorithm
journal: International Journal of Computational Intelligence Systems
brief_summary: This study addresses the prediction and classification of pigmented skin lesions to improve early detection of severe skin diseases like melanoma. The authors utilized the ISIC dataset, applying pre-processing steps including hair removal and median filtering, followed by U-Net segmentation. Features were extracted using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and RGB histogram methods, which were then processed by a Modified Long Short-Term Memory (MLSTM) model optimized via a hybrid SC-STBO metaheuristic algorithm. The system classifies lesions into seven specific categories, including Vascular Lesions, Melanocytic Nevi, and Melanoma. The proposed methodology achieved an accuracy of 0.9931 and a recall of 0.9825, significantly outperforming traditional techniques.

all_task_types.target: Seven specific skin lesion categories (Vascular Lesions, Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Dermatofibroma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, BCC, and Actinic Keratoses)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly performs skin lesion classification into seven discrete diagnostic categories using a Modified Long Short-Term Memory (MLSTM) model. The task is defined as classifying the final skin lesion output into these seven types if the presence of a skin lesion is predicted. Performance is measured using classification metrics such as accuracy (0.9931), recall (0.9825), F1 score, and AUC.

The methodology includes a pre-processing step where U-Net is used for segmentation of the pre-processed images to extract features. This is an intermediate step for the classification task, not the primary prediction output.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (2018 Challenge)
datasets_used.description: The primary dataset used for skin lesion prediction and classification, sourced from the ISIC archive challenge. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions along with corresponding ground truth masks for segmentation.

use.training: Used for training the Modified Long Short-Term Memory (MLSTM) model and optimizing parameters via the SC-STBO algorithm.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed methodology (accuracy and recall metrics reported).
use.other: Ground truth response masks (12,970 total, 5 per image) were used for the U-Net segmentation process.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the methodology's performance in distinguishing true positives from false negatives, specifically to minimize the count of sick people labeled as healthy.
To assess how well the methodology performs in classifying both positive and negative classifications correctly.
To assess how effectively instances are sorted based on prediction likelihood between negative and positive classes, using the ROC curve.
To evaluate the classification performance, particularly considering the importance of recall from a medical standpoint.
Used as a major fitness component for the objective function in the SC-STBO optimization algorithm, alongside accuracy maximization.
Used to generate the Receiver-Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve for AUC calculation.

statistical_methods_used.method: GLCM Texture Features (Contrast, Entropy, Correlation, Variance, Average, Sum Entropy, Difference Entropy, Inertia, Cluster Shade, Cluster Prominence, Homogeneity/ASM, Local Homogeneity/IDM)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To extract textural properties from segmented images for feature vector creation.

statistical_methods_used.method: Color Moments (Mean, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Kurtosis)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To describe color statistics (variation, mean, standard deviation, skewness) from segmented lesion patches across six color spaces.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed method and traditional techniques were made based on metric values (accuracy, recall, F1 score, AUC, RMSE) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Dataset (2018 Challenge).metric: Accuracy, Recall, F1 Score, AUC, RMSE
ISIC Dataset (2018 Challenge).notes: The proposed SC-STBO + MLSTM methodology outperformed traditional techniques (CNN, LSTM) and the STBO-MLSTM baseline. The paper highlights an accuracy of 0.9931 and a recall of 0.9825 as key performance indicators. RMSE values were minimized to 0.0104 for the proposed model at 100 iterations.

architecture_details.feature_input: 4182 characteristics (14 texture features from GLCM, 4096 RGB histogram features, 72 color features)
architecture_details.optimization: Parameters (hidden neurons) tuned by Single Candidate-Oriented Sewing Training-Based Optimizer (SC-STBO) algorithm

training_details.objective_function: Maximize accuracy and minimize RMSE (Root-mean-square error)

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using the DullRazor technique (involving grayscale morphological closure to locate hair, bilinear interpolation to replace hair pixels, and median filtering to smooth replaced pixels)
Median filtering applied to hair-removed images to remove noise such as scratches and air bubbles
Segmentation using the U-Net method to isolate skin lesion regions from the background
conclusions: The proposed methodology, which combines a Modified Long Short-Term Memory (MLSTM) model optimized by a hybrid SC-STBO algorithm with U-Net segmentation and GLCM/RGB feature extraction, effectively predicts and classifies skin lesions into seven categories (Vascular Lesions, Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Dermatofibroma, Benign Keratosis-like Lesions, BCC, and Actinic Keratoses).

The proposed SC-STBO + MLSTM model achieved an accuracy of 0.9931 and a recall of 0.9825 on the ISIC dataset, significantly outperforming traditional techniques such as CNN, LSTM, and the baseline STBO-MLSTM model.
The proposed method demonstrated superior performance in minimizing Root-Mean-Square Error (RMSE), achieving a value of 0.0104 compared to higher RMSE values in comparative models.

The study confirms that the image-based deep learning methodology outperforms pure clinical methodologies and provides effectiveness comparable to board-certified physicians in malignancy categorization.

The integration of the novel hybrid metaheuristic SC-STBO algorithm for parameter optimization in the MLSTM model enhances computational efficiency and classification accuracy for early detection of severe skin diseases like melanoma.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset without reporting external validation on independent datasets, limiting generalizability assessment.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the claimed superiority over baseline models.
The dataset size is small (2,594 images), which may contribute to the unusually high performance metrics (accuracy 0.9931) and raises concerns about overfitting.
The methodology uses a hybrid metaheuristic optimizer (SC-STBO) for parameter tuning, which is a non-standard approach compared to typical gradient-based optimizers in deep learning.
The paper claims the method's effectiveness is 'comparable to board-certified physicians' but does not provide direct comparative clinical trial data or inter-observer agreement statistics.
No data leakage risks are explicitly addressed, and the split between training, validation, and testing sets is not detailed in the provided context.

The feature extraction pipeline (GLCM, RGB histogram, color moments) is computationally intensive and relies on pre-segmentation by U-Net, introducing potential error propagation if segmentation fails.
